title: Making Decisions (If) 🚦
description: Learn to run code only when certain conditions are met using if statements.

slides.title: Crossing the Street 🚶
slides.content:
Right now, our programs simply run from top to bottom, executing every single line.

But what if we want the program to make a decision? For example: 'Only cross the street **IF** the light is green.'

To do this, we use conditional statements called **Control Flow**.

slides.title: The `if` Keyword
slides.content:
The most basic way to make a decision is the `if` statement.

It tests a condition. If the condition is `true`, it runs the code inside it.
If it's `false`, it completely skips the code!

```ruby
if light == "green"
  puts "Walk!"
end
```

slides.title: If in Action
slides.content: Run the code! Notice that `puts "Walk!"` runs, but `puts "Stop!"` does not run because its condition is `false`.
slides.codeSnippet:
light = "green"

if light == "green"
  puts "Walk!"
end

if light == "red"
  puts "Stop!"
end

quizData.question: What is required to close out an `if` block in Ruby?
quizData.explanation: Ruby uses the `end` keyword to know where an `if` block finishes.

slides.title: Testing Conditions 🔬
slides.content:
Remember those boolean operators we learned? They are the heart of `if` statements.

You can check: `==` (equal to), `>` (greater than), `<` (less than), `!=` (not equal), etc.

```ruby
if age >= 18
  puts "You are an adult."
end
```

quizData.question: What happens if the condition after the `if` keyword evaluates to `false`?

options.text: Ruby skips all the code inside that `if` block and moves on.
Ruby runs the code anyway just to be safe.

quizData.explanation: If the condition is false, Ruby acts like that chunk of code doesn't even exist.

slides.title: Indentation 📏
slides.content:
Notice how the code inside an `if` block is pushed to the right (indented) by two spaces? 

Ruby doesn't force you to do this, but it makes seeing the "blocks" infinitely easier to read.
